sections.subtitle: Current Form
sections.text: Milan are in strong form, sitting 2nd in the league with 38 points, having won 3 of their last 5 matches, including a convincing 3-0 victory over Hellas Verona. Genoa, on the other hand, are struggling in 17th place with 15 points, winless in their last 4 matches and conceding consistently, as shown by their streak of 12 games without a clean sheet.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head Record
sections.text: Milan have dominated recent encounters with Genoa, remaining unbeaten in their last 9 meetings, including a 2-1 away win in their most recent clash on May 5, 2025. The last match at San Siro ended in a 0-0 draw, but Milan's historical edge (3 wins, 2 draws in the last 5 head-to-heads) gives them a clear advantage.

sections.subtitle: Key Players and Tactics
sections.text: Milan's attack is led by Rafael Leão (6 goals) and supported by Luka Modrić's creativity (7.61 average rating), though they miss Christian Pulišić due to injury. Genoa rely on Lorenzo Colombo (3 goals) but lack consistency, with defensive vulnerabilities evident in conceding early (first to concede in 5 of 7 recent games).

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics Insight
sections.text: Milan boast a top-tier attack (28 goals, 2nd in the league) and defense (13 conceded, 3rd best), with high pressing (3.47 possessions won in final third per game). Genoa's struggles are clear with 28 goals conceded and poor ball possession (data suggests weaker metrics compared to Milan), making it tough to compete at San Siro.

sections.subtitle: Match Prediction
sections.text: Given Sofascore's prediction (63% home win, 22% draw, 15% away win), Milan's superior form, home advantage, and historical dominance point to a likely victory. Expect Milan to control possession and exploit Genoa's defensive frailties, though Genoa may aim for a late consolation as seen in their goal distribution (high in 76-90 min period).

halftimeSections.subtitle: First half overview
halftimeSections.text: The first half at San Siro saw Genoa take a surprising lead with a well-taken goal by Lorenzo Colombo in the 28th minute. Milan, despite dominating possession and creating more entries into the final third, failed to convert their chances, missing a big opportunity and hitting the woodwork once. Genoa capitalized on their limited but effective attacking play to secure a narrow advantage. The home side showed intent but lacked the finishing touch, while Genoa demonstrated clinical efficiency.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Home team performance
halftimeSections.text: Milan controlled much of the game with a higher share of possession and significantly more entries into the penalty area. Their attacking players, particularly Rafael Leão, showed threat with multiple shots on target, though they couldn't find the back of the net. The team created a notable chance but missed it, reflecting a need for better composure in front of goal. Defensively, they were solid for the most part, though they were caught out for Genoa's goal. Midfielders like Adrien Rabiot and Davide Bartesaghi contributed with key passes, indicating creativity in build-up play.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Away team performance
halftimeSections.text: Genoa played a more conservative game in the first half, focusing on defensive organization and counter-attacking opportunities. Their approach paid off with Colombo's goal, showcasing their ability to strike decisively. While they had fewer entries into the final third compared to Milan, their defensive structure held firm, with multiple clearances from their backline. Goalkeeper Nicola Leali also played a crucial role in keeping Milan at bay. Genoa's overall game plan relied on soaking up pressure and exploiting rare chances.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attack momentum analysis
halftimeSections.text: The game began with Genoa showing early intent, pushing forward in the opening minutes with a brief spell of attacking pressure. However, Milan quickly took control, dominating the momentum for large stretches of the half with sustained periods of attacking play, especially between the 6th and 11th minutes, and again towards the latter stages from the 31st minute onwards. Genoa had their moments, particularly around the time of their goal, with a noticeable shift in momentum just before and after scoring. Overall, Milan dictated the tempo for most of the half, but Genoa made their limited bursts count.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key areas for second half
halftimeSections.text: For Milan, the focus will be on converting their dominance into goals. They need to be more clinical in the final third, capitalizing on the chances they create. Players like Rafael Leão and Christian Pulišić will be key to breaking down Genoa's defense. Genoa, on the other hand, will likely continue their defensive approach, looking to protect their lead while seeking counter-attacking opportunities. Their ability to remain compact and frustrate Milan will be crucial. Substitutions could play a role, with fresh legs potentially shifting the balance for either side.
